title: What Should An AI Device Look Like? — With Alex Himel
summary: Alex Himel, Meta's VP of Wearables, argues that smart glasses represent the next major computing platform and that AI assistants can enhance daily tasks, meetings, and fitness without disrupting user attention. The discussion covers Meta's Ray-Ban glasses strategy, competition from OpenAI, Google, Apple, and Amazon, and unresolved challenges around on-device AI, privacy, and facial recognition.
writeup: Alex Himel, Meta's head of wearables, makes the product case for Ray-Ban Meta glasses as the primary AI wearable platform, arguing that the winning form factor is one people already wear for non-AI reasons. The interview moves through a detailed day-in-the-life scenario (run tracking, hands-free ordering, meeting notes, in-conversation memory recall), then works backward to the design thesis: glasses are the right chassis because, like a broken escalator that becomes stairs, they retain value even when the AI is off. Meta frames the next horizon as personal superintelligence, defined as AI that helps users become the best version of themselves, delivered continuously through a device they would wear anyway.

key_points: Meta's form-factor thesis is that AI devices win by being things people already wear: Ray-Ban Wayfarers are the most popular glasses brand and style in the world, so the hardware earns a place on the face before any AI runs.

Himel uses an 'escalator becomes stairs' analogy to articulate why glasses beat novelty wearables: even dead or off, they still function as sunglasses or optical glasses, whereas a clip-on device with no analog utility disappears from adoption.

Several capabilities Himel describes as 'already live' include auto-captured run videos, fitness stat queries by voice, and calendar lookups while driving, distinguishing them from the near-future shopping orders and auto-sent meeting notes that are not yet shipped.

Meta explicitly rejects the leapfrog approach taken by other AI device companies, arguing that ignoring the incremental path is why prior attempts in the space have failed to create durable use-case clarity.

Personal superintelligence, as Meta frames it, is not about raw capability but about empowering the individual user to be the best version of themselves, a definition that positions the glasses as a persistent personal companion rather than a general-purpose assistant.

Himel distinguishes glasses from smart speakers and laptop AI by pointing to context continuity: glasses see what the user sees and hear what the user hears across all settings, while every other device is stationary or single-purpose.
